# buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com -- Full Content

> Last updated: 2026-05-01

This file contains the full text content of all pages on buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com.
Part of the Open Bankruptcy Project (openbankruptcyproject.org).


---

## Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Albuquerque, NM | buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com - House After BK
Source: https://buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com/cities/albuquerque.html

buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
FHA Loan
VA Loan
Conventional
Waiting Periods
FAQ
By City

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Albuquerque, NM

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Albuquerque, NM. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Albuquerque, NM

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Albuquerque

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Albuquerque, New Mexico, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Albuquerque area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Albuquerque lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Albuquerque Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Albuquerque, New Mexico

Many Albuquerque area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: New Mexico may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Albuquerque housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Albuquerque

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Albuquerque area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Albuquerque often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your Car ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

Home ·
FHA Loan ·
VA Loan ·
Conventional ·
Waiting Periods ·
FAQ ·
By City

This site provides general information only and does not constitute legal advice. No attorney-client relationship is created by using this site. Consult a licensed attorney for advice on your specific situation.
A project of the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit.

## Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Atlanta, GA | buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com - House After BK

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Atlanta, GA

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Atlanta, GA. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Atlanta, GA

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Atlanta

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Atlanta, Georgia, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Atlanta area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Atlanta lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Atlanta Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Atlanta, Georgia

Many Atlanta area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Georgia may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Atlanta housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Atlanta

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Atlanta area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Atlanta often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Austin, TX

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Austin, TX. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Austin, TX

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Austin

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Austin, Texas, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Austin area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Austin lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Austin Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Austin, Texas

Many Austin area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Texas may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Austin housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Austin

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Austin area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Austin often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Baltimore, MD

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Baltimore, MD. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Baltimore, MD

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Baltimore

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Baltimore, Maryland, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Baltimore area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Baltimore lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Baltimore Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Baltimore, Maryland

Many Baltimore area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Maryland may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Baltimore housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Baltimore

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Baltimore area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Baltimore often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Boston, MA

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Boston, MA. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Boston, MA

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Boston

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Boston, Massachusetts, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Boston area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Boston lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Boston Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Boston, Massachusetts

Many Boston area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Massachusetts may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Boston housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Boston

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Boston area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Boston often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Charlotte, NC

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Charlotte, NC. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Charlotte, NC

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Charlotte

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Charlotte, North Carolina, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Charlotte area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Charlotte lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Many Charlotte area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: North Carolina may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Charlotte housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Charlotte

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Charlotte area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Charlotte often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Chicago, IL

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Chicago, IL. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Chicago, IL

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Chicago

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Chicago, Illinois, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Chicago area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Chicago lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Chicago Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Chicago, Illinois

Many Chicago area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Illinois may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Chicago housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Chicago

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Chicago area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Chicago often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your Car ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

## Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Cincinnati, OH | buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com - House After BK
Source: https://buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com/cities/cincinnati.html

buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
FHA Loan
VA Loan
Conventional
Waiting Periods
FAQ
By City

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Cincinnati, OH

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Cincinnati, OH. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Cincinnati, OH

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Cincinnati

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Cincinnati, Ohio, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Cincinnati area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Cincinnati lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Cincinnati Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Cincinnati, Ohio

Many Cincinnati area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Ohio may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Cincinnati housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Cincinnati

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Cincinnati area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Cincinnati often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your Car ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

Finding a Lender in Cleveland

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Cleveland area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Cleveland often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Columbus

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Dallas

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Dallas often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Denver

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Denver, Colorado, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Denver area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in El Paso, TX

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in El Paso, TX. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › El Paso, TX

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in El Paso

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in El Paso, Texas, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the El Paso area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in El Paso, Texas

Many El Paso area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Texas may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the El Paso housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in El Paso

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the El Paso area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in El Paso often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your Car ·
Keep Your House ·

## Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Fort Worth, TX | buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com - House After BK
Source: https://buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com/cities/fort-worth.html

buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
FHA Loan
VA Loan
Conventional
Waiting Periods
FAQ
By City

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Fort Worth, TX

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Fort Worth, TX. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Fort Worth, TX

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Fort Worth

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Fort Worth, Texas, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Fort Worth area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Fort Worth lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Fort Worth Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Fort Worth, Texas

Many Fort Worth area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Texas may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Fort Worth housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Fort Worth

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Fort Worth area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Fort Worth often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Fresno, CA

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Fresno, CA. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Fresno, CA

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Fresno

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Fresno, California, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Fresno area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Fresno lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Fresno, California

Many Fresno area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: California may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Fresno housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Fresno

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Fresno area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Fresno often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Houston, TX

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Houston, TX. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Houston, TX

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Houston

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Houston, Texas, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Houston area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Houston lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Houston Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Houston, Texas

Many Houston area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Texas may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Houston housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Houston

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Houston area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Houston often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Indianapolis, IN

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Indianapolis, IN. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Indianapolis, IN

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Indianapolis

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Indianapolis, Indiana, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Indianapolis area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Indianapolis lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Indianapolis Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Indianapolis, Indiana

Many Indianapolis area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Indiana may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Indianapolis housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Indianapolis

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Indianapolis area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Indianapolis often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

## Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Las Vegas, NV | buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com - House After BK
Source: https://buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com/cities/las-vegas.html

buyahouseafterbankruptcy.com
☰

Home
FHA Loan
VA Loan
Conventional
Waiting Periods
FAQ
By City

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Las Vegas, NV

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Las Vegas, NV. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Las Vegas, NV

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Las Vegas

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Las Vegas, Nevada, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Las Vegas area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Las Vegas lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Las Vegas Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Las Vegas, Nevada

Many Las Vegas area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Nevada may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Las Vegas housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Las Vegas

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Las Vegas area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Las Vegas often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Bankruptcy Tools Network:

Discharge Screener ·
Research Platform ·
Exemptions by State ·
Keep Your Car ·
Keep Your House ·
Bankruptcy Cost ·
File Without a Lawyer ·
Rebuild Credit

Free bankruptcy education. Not legal advice.

Home ·
FHA Loan ·
VA Loan ·
Conventional ·
Waiting Periods ·
FAQ ·
By City

This site provides general information only and does not constitute legal advice. No attorney-client relationship is created by using this site. Consult a licensed attorney for advice on your specific situation.
A project of the Open Bankruptcy Project, a 501(c)(3) nonprofit.

---

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Los Angeles, CA

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Los Angeles, CA. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Los Angeles, CA

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Los Angeles

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Los Angeles, California, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Los Angeles area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Los Angeles lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

Local programs: Check with the Los Angeles housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Los Angeles

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Los Angeles area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Los Angeles often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Louisville, KY

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Louisville, KY. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Louisville, KY

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Louisville

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Louisville, Kentucky, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Louisville area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Louisville lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Louisville Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Down Payment Assistance in Louisville, Kentucky

Many Louisville area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Kentucky may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Louisville housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Louisville

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Louisville area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Louisville often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Memphis, TN

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Memphis, TN. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Memphis, TN

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Memphis

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Memphis, Tennessee, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Memphis area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Memphis lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Memphis Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Tennessee may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Memphis housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Memphis

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Memphis area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Memphis often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Mesa, AZ

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Mesa, AZ. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Mesa, AZ

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Mesa

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Mesa, Arizona, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Mesa area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Mesa lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

USDA3 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval)

Extenuating circumstances: If your bankruptcy was caused by a one-time event outside your control (medical emergency, job loss due to employer closure), you may qualify for shortened waiting periods - as little as 1 year for FHA and VA loans.

Credit Score Requirements for Mesa Homebuyers

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

VA loans: No official minimum credit score, but most lenders require 620+. Available to eligible veterans and active military.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Most people who rebuild their credit after bankruptcy can reach a 620-680 credit score within 18-24 months of discharge. Steps to get there:

Get a secured credit card immediately after discharge and use it for small purchases

Make every payment on time - payment history is 35% of your credit score

Keep credit utilization below 30% (ideally below 10%)

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors

Many Mesa area homebuyers qualify for down payment assistance programs that can make homeownership more accessible after bankruptcy:

FHA loans: Only 3.5% down required. On a $250,000 home, that is $8,750.

State programs: Arizona may offer first-time homebuyer assistance, grants, or below-market rate mortgages through its state housing finance agency.

Local programs: Check with the Mesa housing authority for city-specific down payment assistance, closing cost grants, or community development programs.

Employer programs: Some employers offer down payment assistance as a benefit.

Tip: Even if you owned a home before bankruptcy, you may qualify as a "first-time homebuyer" under many assistance programs if you have not owned a home in the past 3 years.

Finding a Lender in Mesa

Not all lenders are equally willing to work with post-bankruptcy borrowers. In the Mesa area, focus on:

FHA-approved lenders: These are experienced with post-bankruptcy applications and have clear guidelines.

Credit unions: Local credit unions in Mesa often have more flexible underwriting than large national banks.

Mortgage brokers: A broker can shop multiple lenders for you, which is especially valuable when you have a bankruptcy on your record.

HUD-approved housing counselors: Free counseling available through HUD. Counselors can help you develop a plan and connect with appropriate lenders.

Get pre-approved before house hunting so you know exactly what you can afford. Pre-approval also shows sellers you are a serious buyer.

Buy a House After Bankruptcy in Milwaukee, WI

Guide to buying a house after bankruptcy in Milwaukee, WI. FHA waiting periods, local lenders, credit rebuilding, and down payment programs.

Home › Cities › Milwaukee, WI

Buying a House After Bankruptcy in Milwaukee

If you filed bankruptcy and want to buy a home in Milwaukee, Wisconsin, you are not alone. Thousands of people in the Milwaukee area buy homes after bankruptcy every year. The key is understanding the waiting periods, rebuilding your credit, and knowing your loan options.

Bankruptcy does not permanently prevent you from getting a mortgage. Every major loan program has a defined waiting period after discharge, and after that period, you are evaluated on your current credit profile - not your past bankruptcy.

Good news: FHA loans require as little as 2 years after a Chapter 7 discharge. Some Milwaukee lenders specialize in post-bankruptcy mortgages.

Waiting Periods by Loan Type

Here are the standard waiting periods after bankruptcy discharge for major mortgage programs:

Loan TypeAfter Chapter 7After Chapter 13

FHA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

VA2 years from discharge1 year into plan (with court approval) or 2 years after discharge

Conventional (Fannie/Freddie)4 years from discharge2 years from discharge or 4 years from dismissal

After the waiting period, lenders evaluate your current credit profile. Here is what you need:

FHA loans: Minimum 580 credit score for 3.5% down payment (500-579 requires 10% down). FHA is the most common post-bankruptcy path.

Conventional loans: Minimum 620, but 680+ gets better rates. Typically requires 5-20% down payment.

Do not apply for multiple accounts at once - spread applications out

Monitor your credit reports and dispute any errors
